author: @sailingoctopus1
text:
There were a couple of errors in what you said, Rob. You said that only nine (sic) years away you were expected to triple your energy consumption, suggesting that in 2030 you would be compelled to buy an electric car, whereas, as you said earlier, 2030 is (was) simply the date we would no longer be able to buy NEW petrol cars. Like many people, you took this date as the date that everyone would have to switch over to electric nine (sic) years from now, but, of course, many people never buy new cars and will continue to drive around in petrol cars for decades.

The second error was to assume that petrol cars consume no electricity, whereas a lot of electricity is consumed in refining and distributing petrol.

Finally, you ignored the fact that a vast proportion of EV charging is done at night at times when, very often, there is an abundance of electricity available, so the burden it places on the grid is not as great as implied. Yes it will need additional generating capacity, but because most of the new grid capacity added in the next twenty years will be from renewables there will be large surpluses at night, perfect for charging all the EVs at low cost and for recharging all the thermal stores used for home heating using heat pumps.

There is no problem with generating sufficient electricity to meet this new demand. We could cover Scotland with wind turbines and generate sufficient electricity for the whole of Europe, not to mention offshore wind farms. Or, we could cover parts of Morocco with solar panels and generate enough electricity for the whole world. In practice, in will probably be a bit of both.

The problem is more one of distribution rather than generation.

What we really need is a cheap source of low temperature superconductors. Perhaps this is something you could investigate, Rob.

author: @maxkennedy5073
text: The self charging car is a bogus repackaging of a hybrid but a part of your discussion was just as bogus.  What was lacking when you talked about the tripling of the electricity usage cost was a comparison to the fossil fuel costs people have running their ICE cars.  Here in Canada there is no doubt that running electric is far less expensive than running on gas and without knowing your costs I bet the same is true there due to the much higher efficiency of electric motors.  The comparison you needed to make regarding cost wasn't to say it would be 10% of your pay but a comparison to the cost if you stayed with an all ICE vehicle.  Just because the energy source has changed doesn't mean it's an increase in energy usage, it's actually a decrease, or overall cost.  Everything else is right on the money, huge increase in electrical consumption if all remains the same regarding individual transportation, huge increase in generation needed, massive investment in infrastructure etc...  However the misrepresentation of a massive cost increase, yes there will be increased prices due to increased electrical demand but then just look at gas prices going up would it really be an increase in overall cost to the household?

author: @FactsnotFiction555
text:
Toyota first introduced the phrase "Self Charging" in order to differentiate its standard hybrid models from the more recently introduced "Plug In Hybrids" and latterly introduced "Full EV" model. That's it, nothing sinister, just a way of marketing the 3 options available.
There are 3 types of hybrid vehicle available and best way is to describe them as "Mild Hybrid" MHEV, "Full Hybrid" HEV and "Plug In Hybrid" PHEV. Clear as mud for those in the know 😂

replies.author: @pedjamilosavljevic6235
replies.text:
Norwegians love electric cars (most Ev's per capita in the world). However , they are still buying Tesla's and other EV's and not Aptera , because most of the time , there are clouds on the sky and northern position of the country doesn't make solar cells very efficient (+ lots of snow in the winter and very short days). Also , they are able to buy such cars , which are pretty expensive , because they sell a lot of natural gas and some oil . If we flip the coin , and put Aptera somewhere close to the equator (where there is a lot of sun all year around and the solar panels are more effective) , I would like to see durability of that car in those parts of the world. 
 Btw , this video is about two years old and since , lots of things has changed in the world , regarding energy , obtaining lithium and other rare metals for the batteries. Battery powered cars will never be a solution (part of). EV's , in general , maybe , if viable and , above all , cheap way of production of "green" hydrogen is adopted , used in a form of metal hydride (pretty controlled substance at the moment) , or something nuclear (small and light enough and very safe in any and all circumstances). 2035 without IC powered transport and other machines is just a pipe dream of EU (GB) bureaucracy (directed from Washington DC) , globalist elites , out of touch with reality.

author: @ericblenner-hassett3945
text: I do not recomend doing it, however, a portable generator connected to the charge controller, with safety geat to allow it to charge an electric while parked, is not dificult to design to be in the car.  The implimentation is where the bigest safety risks are.  The emissions need to be vented out, air in, fuel storage with crash protection, electrical sysstem emergency shutoff are just the edge of the rabbit hole of making a hybrid.  Adding solar cells, roof mounted flat vertical turbines and blocking oscilators and super capacitors to build up a reserve to try and add to the remaining charge is an option.  There are actual nuclear batteries, they are limited in voltage and mili amps out per cell.

author: @saiello2061
text: There's so much technological BS out there, from individual pundits on YouTube, to corporations, to political rhetoric, to academic research, it's not real. Unfortunately too many people don't have the wherewithal to decipher what's real and what isn't. Britain has the resources to be energy self sufficient, there just isn't the political and/or social will, a will thats been derailed by the promises of green energy.
